metadata.title: BusinessDNA — Find and build the business that fits you
metadata.description: AI-powered guidance to discover, validate and launch the business that matches your personality, skills and goals.

common.somethingWentWrong: Something went wrong. Please try again.

hero.title: Your future business starts with the right plan.
hero.subtitle: Personalized business matches, AI-generated plans and step-by-step guidance — built around who you actually are.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.subtitle: From personality to launch, in four guided steps.
howItWorks.step1Desc: Tell us about your skills, goals, budget and lifestyle.
howItWorks.step2Desc: See compatibility scores across dozens of real business models.
howItWorks.step3Desc: A full business plan, financial model and brand direction — generated for you.
howItWorks.step4Desc: Work through a month-by-month plan with your AI Co-Founder.

features.title: Everything you need to go from idea to launch
features.item1Desc: Matches are based on how you actually work, not generic trends.
features.item2Desc: Executive summary, market analysis, SWOT and financial forecast.
features.item3Desc: A daily thinking partner for decisions, not just documents.
features.item4Desc: Month-by-month milestones so you always know the next step.

journey.title: Your journey
journey.subtitle: One guided path from uncertainty to launch day.

testimonials.title: Built for people building something real

t1.quote: The blueprint gave me a real starting point instead of a blank page.
t1.name: Andrei P.

t2.quote: I finally had a plan that matched how I actually want to work.
t2.name: Maria I.

t3.quote: The roadmap kept me moving instead of stuck in research mode.

pricing.title: Simple, honest pricing
pricing.subtitle: Start free. Upgrade when you're ready to launch.
pricing.proDesc: Full blueprints, roadmaps and your AI Co-Founder.

q1.question: Do I need a business idea to start?
q1.answer: No — most people start with the assessment and let BusinessDNA suggest matches based on their profile.

q2.question: What languages are supported?
q2.answer: The app and every generated document are available in English and Romanian, with more languages planned.

q3.question: Can I cancel anytime?
q3.answer: Yes, subscriptions are month-to-month with no long-term commitment.

q4.question: Is my data kept private?
q4.answer: Your assessment answers and business data are never shared or sold to third parties.

cta.title: Find the business that fits you.
cta.subtitle: It takes about ten minutes to get your first matches.

intro.title: Let's get to know you.
intro.subtitle: Eight short sections, one question at a time. Answer honestly — there are no wrong answers, only better matches.

validation.required: This one needs an answer before you continue.

review.title: Let's review your answers.
review.subtitle: Everything here is editable — tap any section to change your answer.

reveal.title: Your Entrepreneur DNA
reveal.subtitle: We've analyzed thousands of data points from your answers.

hero.summary: Based on your answers so far, you show strong alignment with {archetype} traits — comfort with ambiguity, a pull toward new ideas, and a bias for bold moves over safe ones.

systemsBuilder.title: Systems Builder
systemsBuilder.description: You turn ideas into processes that keep running without you in the room.
systemsBuilder.summary: You think in workflows and checklists more than grand gestures — your edge comes from building things that hold up under repetition, not just from a single good idea.

visionaryOperator.title: Visionary Operator
visionaryOperator.description: You pair a long view of where things are headed with the discipline to actually run the day-to-day.
visionaryOperator.summary: You're rare in combining big-picture thinking with operational follow-through — you can set the direction and still show up to execute it.

creativeStrategist.title: Creative Strategist
creativeStrategist.description: You lead with original ideas, then work backward into a plan that makes them real.
creativeStrategist.summary: Your instinct is to imagine the unconventional option first, then apply structure — that ordering is what tends to set your work apart.

growthArchitect.title: Growth Architect
growthArchitect.description: You're drawn to finding the mechanisms that make a business compound, not just start.
growthArchitect.summary: You naturally look past the launch toward what makes something scale — the channel, the loop, the second and third order effect.

executionSpecialist.title: Execution Specialist
executionSpecialist.description: You're most energized by shipping — turning a plan into something real, fast.
executionSpecialist.summary: Where others deliberate, you tend to move — your strength is closing the gap between a decision and a finished result.

dnaProfile.sectionSubtitle: How your answers map across all seven founder archetypes.

builder.name: The Builder
builder.description: Drawn to shipping tangible things and iterating in public.

visionary.name: The Visionary
visionary.description: Sees where a market is headed before it gets there.

operator.name: The Operator
operator.description: Turns messy processes into repeatable systems.

creator.name: The Creator
creator.description: Leads with original ideas, content, and craft.

seller.name: The Seller
seller.description: Energized by pitching, negotiating, and closing.

leader.name: The Leader
leader.description: Rallies people around a shared goal.

analyst.name: The Analyst
analyst.description: Grounds decisions in data before committing.

items.strategicVision: Strategic vision — you naturally think several moves ahead.
items.adaptability: Adaptability — you adjust quickly when plans change.
items.riskAppetite: Risk appetite — uncertainty doesn't stop you from moving.
items.clearCommunication: Clear communication — you make complex ideas easy to follow.
items.fastLearning: Fast learning — you pick up new domains quickly.

items.delegation: Delegation — handing off ownership instead of holding on to everything.
items.financialDiscipline: Financial discipline — tighter tracking of budget and burn.
items.technicalDepth: Technical depth — going deeper on the tools behind the idea.
items.consistentExecution: Consistent execution — following through past the first burst of energy.
items.networkBuilding: Network building — investing in relationships before you need them.

workStyle.sectionSubtitle: How you tend to operate day-to-day.

risk.label: Risk Tolerance
risk.description: How comfortable you are making decisions without full certainty.

leadership.label: Leadership
leadership.description: How naturally you take charge and direct others.

communicationStyle.label: Communication
communicationStyle.description: How you prefer to explain ideas and give feedback.

technicalAbility.label: Technical Ability
technicalAbility.description: Your comfort working directly with technical tools and systems.

creativity.label: Creativity
creativity.description: How often you default to a novel approach over a proven one.

automationAffinity.label: Automation Affinity
automationAffinity.description: How much you lean on tools and automation instead of manual effort.

businessExperience.label: Business Experience
businessExperience.description: How much hands-on experience you bring from prior ventures or roles.

timeAvailability.label: Time Availability
timeAvailability.description: How many hours a week you can realistically commit right now.

opportunities.sectionSubtitle: A first look at the kinds of businesses that could fit your profile.
opportunities.disclaimer: Illustrative example — your real matches are calculated once your assessment is fully processed.

sample1.name: Local Service Marketplace App
sample1.description: Illustrative example — connects nearby customers with independent service providers.

sample2.name: Niche Subscription Box
sample2.description: Illustrative example — a recurring-revenue product curated for a specific audience.

sample3.name: B2B Workflow SaaS Tool
sample3.description: Illustrative example — software that automates a repetitive task for other businesses.

whyTheseMatches.body: These placeholder examples were chosen to illustrate how compatibility, difficulty, and scalability will be presented once real matching is in place.

reasons.reason1: Your DNA profile leans toward vision and creativity, which tends to fit fast-moving, idea-driven businesses.
reasons.reason2: Your work style shows solid risk tolerance, which opens the door to less conventional business models.
reasons.reason3: Your available time and business experience shape how ambitious a starting point makes sense.

thinking.subtext: This usually takes about twenty seconds.

aboutYou.title: About you
aboutYou.description: The basics — so we can ground everything else in your real situation.

financialSituation.title: Financial situation
financialSituation.description: What you have to invest, and what you need to earn.

personality.title: Personality
personality.description: How you think, decide and work best.

skills.title: Skills
skills.description: Rate your comfort across ten common founder skills.

lifestyle.title: Lifestyle
lifestyle.description: The shape of the working life you actually want.

risk.title: Risk
risk.description: How much uncertainty you can comfortably carry.

interests.title: Interests
interests.description: What draws you in — and what you'd rather avoid.

vision.title: Vision
vision.description: Where this is all headed, in your own words.

country.label: Which country are you based in?

weeklyAvailability.label: How many hours a week could you realistically give this?

incomeUrgency.label: How urgently do you need this to replace your income?

options.low: Not urgent — I can be patient
options.moderate: Somewhat — I'd like traction within a year
options.high: Fairly urgent — within a few months

budget.label: How much could you invest to get started?

targetMonthlyIncome.label: What monthly income would make this feel worth it?

desiredTimeline.label: How soon would you like to see real traction?

decisionMaking.label: How do you usually make important decisions?

options.collaborative: I talk it through with others
options.decisive: I decide fast and adjust later

sellingPreference.label: How comfortable are you selling to people directly?

leadership.label: How comfortable are you leading and directing others?

problemSolving.label: How much do you enjoy solving messy, undefined problems?

creativity.label: How creative would you say you are?

structureVsFlexibility.label: Where do you sit between structure and flexibility?
structureVsFlexibility.description: 0 means you crave structure and routine, 100 means you thrive on flexibility and change.

remote.label: Where would you like to work from?

travel.label: How much travel would you be comfortable with?

employees.label: Do you want to manage a team?

options.soloOnly: Just me, at least at first

freedom.label: How much day-to-day freedom do you need?

workingHours.label: How many hours a week do you want to work, long-term?

b2bVsB2c.label: Who would you rather sell to?

riskTolerance.label: How much financial risk are you willing to take on?

failureTolerance.label: How would a failed first attempt affect you?
failureTolerance.description: 1 means it would be very discouraging, 5 means you'd see it as part of the process.

investmentConfidence.label: How confident are you investing your own money into this?

businessModels.label: Which of these business models appeal to you?

topics.label: Anything else you're drawn to that we haven't covered?

neverDo.label: Is there anything you'd never want your day-to-day work to involve?

fiveYearVision.label: Where do you want this to have taken you in five years?

definitionOfSuccess.label: What would make this feel like a success to you, personally?

resources.book1: Book: "The E-Myth Accountant" by Michael E. Gerber & J. C. Hair
resources.community1: Community: r/accounting and the QuickBooks Accountant community forum

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes steady retainer-client growth, not a viral spike — typical of referral-driven accounting practices.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — actual break-even depends on client-acquisition pace.

grossMargin.notes: Estimate once a part-time bookkeeper is on staff; solo-founder margins run higher.

netMargin.notes: After software, insurance, continuing education, and staff wages.

scenarios.conservative: 6 retainer clients at the base tier, slow referral flow.
scenarios.expected: 12 retainer clients across mixed tiers plus seasonal tax filing income.
scenarios.optimistic: 18+ retainer clients plus an active fractional-CFO engagement.

risks.risk1: A large client leaving mid-year creates an immediate revenue gap.
risks.risk2: Tax-season staffing costs spike faster than tax-season revenue.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Model revenue growth as linear and retainer-based, not exponential.

positioning.statement: The outsourced finance department for founders who'd rather build their business than reconcile their books.

marketing.brandPromise: Your books close in 5 business days, every month, without you lifting a finger.

uvp.statement: Certified ProAdvisor bookkeeping with transparent flat-tier pricing and a guaranteed close date.
uvp.diff2: Flat, tiered pricing with no surprise invoices

icp.description: Small businesses and startups with 2-50 employees who have outgrown a spreadsheet but aren't ready for an in-house controller.
icp.firmographics: €200k-€3M annual revenue, service or e-commerce businesses.

persona1.name: Busy startup founder
persona1.description: Founder who's been doing their own books in a spreadsheet and just closed a funding round.

persona2.name: Established SMB owner
persona2.description: Owner of a 10-year-old business frustrated with their current bookkeeper's slow turnaround.

painPoints.point1: Books are always weeks behind, so decisions are made on stale numbers.
painPoints.point2: Surprise tax bills because no one was tracking quarterly estimates.

outcomes.outcome1: Know the real cash position at any moment, not just at year-end.
outcomes.outcome2: Never miss a filing deadline or owe a surprise penalty.

messaging.core: Stop chasing your books. Let them close themselves, on time, every month.
messaging.support1: Certified ProAdvisor bookkeepers, not a call-center.

contentPillars.pillar1: Small-business tax deadlines and compliance reminders

marketing.channelStrategy: Referrals from lawyers, business consultants, and existing clients are primary; local-SEO content and chamber-of-commerce sponsorships build secondary awareness.

community.description: A small, curated referral network of complementary professionals (lawyers, business consultants).

stage1.name: Referral introduction
stage1.description: A trusted contact introduces the prospect.

stage2.name: Books-health assessment
stage2.description: Free review of current books and pain points.

stage3.name: Tiered proposal
stage3.description: A flat-fee proposal matched to transaction volume.

retention.tactic1: Quarterly financial review call included in every retainer.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Keep every message grounded in trust and accuracy, never hype.

objectives.0: Get certified as a QuickBooks/Xero ProAdvisor.
objectives.1: Register with the local chartered accounting body.

deliverables.0: Signed professional indemnity insurance policy.

checklist.0: Confirm professional license is active.
checklist.1: Set up a client document portal.

commonMistakes.0: Skipping professional indemnity insurance to save money early.

successCriteria.0: Licensing and insurance are both active.

aiRecommendations.0: Prioritize ProAdvisor certification before any paid marketing.

objectives.0: Sign 2-3 paying retainer clients.

deliverables.0: Completed books-health assessments for early prospects.

checklist.0: Time the first month-end close against the 5-day promise.

commonMistakes.0: Discounting so deeply the retainer isn't sustainable.

successCriteria.0: At least 2 clients retained past their first quarter.

aiRecommendations.0: Ask each validation client for one structured piece of feedback.

objectives.0: Standardize the month-end close checklist.

deliverables.0: Documented standard operating procedure for onboarding.

checklist.0: Store the checklist inside the practice-management tool.

commonMistakes.0: Customizing the process per client instead of standardizing.

successCriteria.0: Every active client follows the same close checklist.

aiRecommendations.0: Use the same onboarding checklist for every new client to keep quality consistent.

objectives.0: Publicly announce the practice through the referral network.

deliverables.0: Updated professional profiles (LinkedIn, chamber of commerce directory).

checklist.0: Confirm the client portal and invoicing flow are tested end to end.

commonMistakes.0: Launching before the onboarding flow has been tested with a real client.

successCriteria.0: First public-facing referral conversation booked.

aiRecommendations.0: Ask every early client for exactly one warm introduction.

objectives.0: Deliver a flawless first month-end close.

deliverables.0: Signed engagement letter and onboarding checklist completed.

checklist.0: Set clear document-submission deadlines with the client.

commonMistakes.0: Overpromising turnaround time to win the client.

successCriteria.0: First client renews past month three.

aiRecommendations.0: Automate the document-reminder emails from day one.

objectives.0: Reach 10 retainer clients with under 10% monthly churn.

deliverables.0: Client segmentation by transaction volume and service tier.

checklist.0: Introduce a minimum-transaction-volume qualification for new clients.

commonMistakes.0: Accepting every prospect regardless of fit.

successCriteria.0: Retainer revenue exceeds the founder's target monthly income.

aiRecommendations.0: Flag low-fit prospects automatically based on transaction volume.

objectives.0: Hire the first part-time bookkeeper.

deliverables.0: Documented training plan for new hires.

checklist.0: Cross-train the new hire on bookkeeping and payroll.

commonMistakes.0: Hiring reactively during the tax-season crunch.

successCriteria.0: New hire independently closes books for at least 5 clients.

aiRecommendations.0: Use AI-assisted receipt categorization to free up the new hire's time for review work.

objectives.0: Launch the fractional-CFO service tier.

deliverables.0: Distinct fractional-CFO service agreement and pricing sheet.

checklist.0: Price the CFO tier separately from basic bookkeeping.

commonMistakes.0: Pricing the CFO tier the same as basic bookkeeping.

successCriteria.0: At least one client upgrades to the CFO tier.

aiRecommendations.0: Identify upgrade candidates automatically from revenue-growth patterns in client books.

objectives.0: Expand into an adjacent vertical, such as e-commerce seller bookkeeping.

deliverables.0: Vertical-specific onboarding checklist.

checklist.0: Validate the new vertical's software integrations before marketing to it.

commonMistakes.0: Expanding into a vertical without adapting onboarding.

successCriteria.0: First client signed in the new vertical.

aiRecommendations.0: Reuse the existing close checklist as the base for the new vertical's playbook.

objectives.0: Package the client book and SOPs for acquisition by a larger firm.

deliverables.0: Documented SOPs for every recurring client workflow.

checklist.0: Confirm every client relationship is documented, not founder-dependent.

commonMistakes.0: Keeping undocumented, founder-only client relationships.

successCriteria.0: Client book transfers cleanly to a new owner in due diligence.

aiRecommendations.0: Use the accumulated close-time data as evidence of operational quality for buyers.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Growth is retainer-driven and gradual — do not suggest viral growth tactics.

item1.title: The E-Myth Accountant
item1.description: Michael E. Gerber & J. C. Hair on building a scalable accounting practice, not just doing the work.

item2.title: QuickBooks Online ProAdvisor Certification
item2.description: Free, official certification from Intuit that unlocks the ProAdvisor directory listing.

item3.title: r/accounting
item3.description: Active practitioner community for practice-management and technical questions.

item4.title: The Successful Bookkeeper Podcast
item4.description: Practice-growth interviews aimed specifically at bookkeeping firm owners.

item5.title: AccountingWEB Daily Digest
item5.description: Daily regulatory and tax-code change alerts for practitioners.

item6.title: QuickBooks Online Accountant
item6.description: The core practice-management and client bookkeeping platform.

item7.title: Dext
item7.description: Receipt and invoice capture with OCR, feeding directly into the ledger.

item8.title: Dext AI Categorization
item8.description: AI-assisted line-item categorization that speeds up monthly close.

item9.title: Chartered/Certified Public Accountant credential
item9.description: The core professional credential clients look for when vetting a firm.

item10.title: QuickBooks Connect
item10.description: Annual conference for accounting and bookkeeping practice owners.

item11.title: Local chartered accounting body mentor program
item11.description: Pairs new practice owners with an experienced mentor in the same professional body.

item12.title: Month-end close checklist
item12.description: The standardized checklist used to guarantee the 5-day close.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Favor practitioner-credibility resources (certifications, professional bodies) over generic business content.

item1.title: Selling a fractional-CFO tier to your best existing retainer clients is cheaper than acquiring new ones.

item2.title: E-commerce sellers are chronically underserved by generalist bookkeepers and pay a premium for inventory-aware accounting.

item1.title: A single missed quarterly estimated-tax deadline for a client can end the relationship instantly, regardless of your track record.
Pricing by the hour instead of by transaction volume, which punishes efficiency.

item2.title: Taking on clients in industries the firm doesn't understand (e.g., inventory-heavy retail) without adjusting the process.

item1.title: Offering a free books-health assessment converts warm referrals faster than any cold outreach.
Standardize the close checklist before hiring — otherwise every new hire reinvents the process.
Cap any single client at under 20% of monthly recurring revenue to avoid concentration risk.
Firms that publish their close-date guarantee publicly close more referral deals than those that don't.
Most small-business owners switch accountants not over price, but over slow response times.
Continuing-education credit renewal fees are easy to forget when budgeting the first year.
Demand for e-commerce and multi-currency bookkeeping is rising faster than demand for traditional single-location retail bookkeeping.
A one-person practice reached 25 retainer clients within 2 years by referral alone, with zero paid advertising.
A practice that took on too many low-margin, high-transaction-volume clients burned out its only bookkeeper within a year.

q1.question: Do I need a physical office to start?
q1.answer: No — most client work can be done remotely through a cloud practice-management portal.

myth1.myth: AI will make bookkeepers obsolete within a few years.
myth1.fact: AI automates the transactional categorization work, but advisory judgment and client trust remain human-led for the foreseeable future.

item1.title: Send document-request reminders automatically 5 business days before the close deadline.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Keep all guidance at the level of running/growing the firm — never give specific tax or accounting advice for an end client's actual filing.

resources.course1: Course: CFP (Certified Financial Planner) certification program
resources.community1: Community: Financial Planning Association (FPA) local chapter
resources.book1: Book: "The Million-Dollar Financial Advisor" by David J. Mullen Jr.
resources.checklist1: Checklist: Annual comprehensive plan refresh checklist

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes slow early growth that compounds as AUM and referrals build over years, not a fast ramp.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — actual break-even depends heavily on referral-network development speed.

grossMargin.notes: High margin once AUM is established, since incremental client servicing cost is low.

netMargin.notes: After compliance, insurance, and software costs.

scenarios.conservative: A handful of early clients, AUM still below the first meaningful threshold.
scenarios.expected: Steady referral flow, AUM past €5M with a mix of AUM and flat-fee clients.
scenarios.optimistic: Strong centers-of-influence network and a junior advisor generating additional fee-split revenue.

risks.risk1: A prolonged market downturn reduces AUM-based fee revenue across the whole client base at once.
risks.risk2: Regulatory or licensing changes can require costly compliance updates.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Model revenue as slow-building and AUM-dependent, sensitive to market cycles.

positioning.statement: Financial plans built for decades, not quarters — fee-only, fiduciary advice with no commission conflicts.

marketing.brandPromise: A plan built around your goals, reviewed every quarter, with no commissions clouding the advice.

uvp.statement: CFP-certified, fee-only fiduciary planning with a personalized quarterly review cadence.
uvp.diff1: Fee-only — no commission conflicts of interest

icp.description: Pre-retirees and business owners planning an exit, typically aged 45-65 with meaningful investable assets.
icp.firmographics: €250k+ investable assets or a business exit event on the horizon.

persona1.name: Pre-retiree planner
persona1.description: Someone 5-10 years from retirement, unsure if their savings are on track.

persona2.name: Business owner planning an exit
persona2.description: Owner preparing to sell their business and needing a plan for the proceeds.

painPoints.point1: Not knowing whether current savings support the retirement lifestyle they want.
painPoints.point2: Distrust of commission-based advisors who may recommend products that benefit the advisor.

outcomes.outcome1: A clear, written plan with a specific retirement-readiness number.
outcomes.outcome2: Confidence that the advice given has no hidden commission incentive.

messaging.core: A plan for the life you actually want to retire into, from an advisor with nothing to sell you but advice.

marketing.channelStrategy: Referrals from accountants and estate attorneys are primary; small educational seminars build secondary trust; long-form content builds experimental organic reach.

community.description: A centers-of-influence network of accountants and estate attorneys who refer clients in both directions.

stage1.name: Referral introduction
stage1.description: A trusted accountant or attorney makes the introduction.

stage3.name: Financial plan proposal
stage3.description: A flat-fee or AUM-based engagement proposal.

retention.tactic1: Quarterly portfolio review meetings, calendared a year in advance.

analytics.metric1: Referral-source conversion rate by professional partner

aiMetadata.generationHints: Never generate urgency-driven or scarcity-driven messaging — this audience buys on trust over years.

objectives.0: Obtain CFP certification and complete regulatory licensing.

deliverables.0: Signed E&O insurance policy and compliance program documentation.

checklist.0: Confirm licensing is approved in every jurisdiction served.

commonMistakes.0: Underestimating licensing approval timelines.

successCriteria.0: Licensing and compliance program are both active.

aiRecommendations.0: Start the licensing process months before any planned launch date.

objectives.0: Complete financial plans for 3-5 early clients.

deliverables.0: Documented rationale for every early recommendation.

checklist.0: Use the standard plan template even for early, informal clients.

commonMistakes.0: Skipping documentation on early, informal engagements.

successCriteria.0: At least 3 completed, documented financial plans.

aiRecommendations.0: Use plan-drafting software from the very first engagement to build a consistent record.

objectives.0: Standardize the discovery-meeting-to-plan workflow.

deliverables.0: A repeatable financial-plan template in the planning software.

checklist.0: Store the template centrally so every plan starts from the same structure.

commonMistakes.0: Reinventing the plan format for every client.

successCriteria.0: Every new client's plan starts from the standard template.

aiRecommendations.0: Use AI-assisted meeting-note summarization to speed up plan drafting without changing the advice itself.

objectives.0: Announce the practice to the professional referral network.

deliverables.0: Updated professional profiles and a hosted educational seminar.

checklist.0: Confirm compliance documentation is complete before any public announcement.

commonMistakes.0: Launching before compliance paperwork is fully in place.

successCriteria.0: First seminar or referral-network introduction completed.

aiRecommendations.0: Personalize the seminar follow-up outreach per attendee rather than sending a generic blast.

objectives.0: Deliver the first paid comprehensive financial plan.

deliverables.0: Signed engagement letter and completed plan presentation.

checklist.0: Charge the full planning fee, even for the first paying client.

commonMistakes.0: Underpricing the first engagement out of eagerness to win the client.

successCriteria.0: First client signs on for the ongoing quarterly-review retainer.

aiRecommendations.0: Flag portfolio-risk anomalies automatically ahead of each quarterly review.

objectives.0: Reach a stable client base generating the target monthly income.

deliverables.0: A documented values-fit qualification step in the discovery meeting.

checklist.0: Qualify prospects on values fit, not just asset size.

commonMistakes.0: Accepting every prospect regardless of values fit.

item1.title: Business owners approaching an exit are an underserved niche — most advisors focus only on retirees.
A single bad market cycle early in the practice's life can shake client confidence disproportionately to the actual portfolio impact.
Competing on investment returns instead of on planning quality and trust — a race the advisor can't consistently win.
A free, no-obligation discovery meeting converts referral leads faster than any paid marketing.
Co-lead client meetings with a new junior advisor for at least two quarters before any handoff.
Document every recommendation's rationale — it's both good practice and fiduciary protection.
Advisors who host small educational seminars build referral trust faster than those relying on cold outreach.
Most clients choose an advisor based on trust from a referral, not on comparing investment performance across firms.
E&O insurance premiums rise noticeably once AUM crosses certain thresholds — budget for it.
Demand for fee-only fiduciary advisors is rising faster than demand for commission-based ones, as awareness of conflicts of interest grows.
A solo fee-only advisor grew to €8M in AUM within 3 years purely through a centers-of-influence referral network.
An advisor who marketed aggressively on investment returns lost credibility when a market downturn hit within the first year.

myth1.fact: Most client value comes from planning discipline and behavioral coaching, not investment selection skill.

resources.checklist1: Checklist: Annual policy renewal review checklist

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes renewal commissions compound as the book grows, with new-business sales layered on top.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — actual break-even depends on new-business sales pace and carrier appointment speed.

grossMargin.notes: Renewal commissions carry very low servicing cost once the client relationship is established.

netMargin.notes: After AMS, E&O insurance, and carrier-appointment maintenance costs.

scenarios.conservative: A small book of 15-20 personal-lines policies, slow referral flow.
scenarios.expected: 35-40 active policies with steady renewals and a growing referral network.
scenarios.optimistic: A growing commercial-lines book plus a strong personal-lines renewal base.

risks.risk1: Losing a key carrier appointment removes an entire product line overnight.
risks.risk2: Commission-rate compression from carriers reduces per-policy revenue over time.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Model revenue as renewal-compounding on top of new-business sales, not a single revenue curve.

positioning.statement: The right coverage, from someone who actually shops it for you across multiple carriers.

marketing.brandPromise: We shop your coverage across carriers every renewal, so you never overpay for the same protection.

uvp.statement: Independent, multi-carrier comparison shopping with hands-on claims advocacy.
uvp.diff1: Not captive to any single insurer
uvp.diff2: Hands-on claims advocacy, not a call center

icp.description: Families and small-business owners needing personal or commercial coverage who are tired of dealing with a captive single-carrier agent.
icp.firmographics: Homeowners, small-business owners with 1-50 employees, families with life/health coverage needs.

persona1.name: First-time homeowner
persona1.description: Recently purchased a home and needs homeowners and auto coverage bundled.

persona2.name: Small-business owner needing commercial coverage
persona2.description: Owner who needs general liability, property, and workers' comp coverage as the business grows.

painPoints.point1: Feeling locked into one insurer's pricing with no easy way to compare.
painPoints.point2: Slow, impersonal claims support when something actually goes wrong.

outcomes.outcome1: Confidence that they're getting competitive pricing every renewal, not just the first year.
outcomes.outcome2: A real person to call when filing a claim, not a phone tree.

messaging.core: One broker, every carrier — so you always know you're getting the best coverage for the price.

contentPillars.pillar1: Coverage-gap awareness for homeowners and small businesses

marketing.channelStrategy: Referrals from existing clients, real-estate agents, and lenders are primary; local event sponsorship builds secondary awareness; short social posts on coverage gaps are experimental.

community.description: A referral network of real-estate agents and mortgage lenders who need a broker for their clients' insurance requirements.

stage1.name: Referral or life-event trigger
stage1.description: A referral or a life event (new home, new business) prompts the inquiry.

stage2.name: Needs analysis
stage2.description: A conversation to understand actual coverage needs and risk exposure.

stage3.name: Comparative quote presentation

retention.tactic1: Automated renewal re-shopping 60 days before every policy expiration.

analytics.metric1: Renewal retention rate by policy type

objectives.0: Obtain property & casualty and life & health licensing.

successCriteria.0: At least 5 policies bound in the first quarter.

objectives.0: Standardize the needs-analysis and quoting workflow.

commonMistakes.0: Skipping documented needs analysis for simple-seeming policies.

checklist.0: Confirm every planned carrier appointment is live before marketing broadly.

commonMistakes.0: Launching before carrier appointments are fully active.

successCriteria.0: First referral-network introduction meeting completed.

aiRecommendations.0: Personalize outreach to each referral partner based on their typical client profile.

objectives.0: Bind the first paying client's policy and confirm smooth claims support if needed.

deliverables.0: Signed policy and a written renewal-process explanation for the client.

checklist.0: Walk every new client through the renewal process in writing.

commonMistakes.0: Not explaining the renewal process clearly at binding.

successCriteria.0: First client renews without a coverage complaint.

aiRecommendations.0: Set an automated check-in message at the 30-day mark after binding to catch early concerns.

objectives.0: Reach 25-30 active policies with a stable renewal rate.

deliverables.0: Automated renewal-reminder sequences configured in the AMS.

checklist.0: Start renewal-reminder sequences 60 days before expiration for every policy.

commonMistakes.0: Focusing only on new business and neglecting renewal follow-up.

successCriteria.0: Renewal retention rate exceeds 85%.

aiRecommendations.0: Flag policies at risk of non-renewal automatically based on engagement signals.

objectives.0: Cross-sell additional policies to the existing client book.

deliverables.0: A full coverage review completed for every client at renewal.

checklist.0: Review the client's full coverage picture at every renewal, not just the expiring policy.

commonMistakes.0: Never revisiting existing clients' coverage needs after the initial sale.

successCriteria.0: At least 1 in 4 renewals results in an additional policy sold.

aiRecommendations.0: Surface cross-sell opportunities automatically based on life-event or business-growth signals.

objectives.0: Launch a dedicated commercial-lines practice for higher-premium accounts.

deliverables.0: A distinct commercial-lines needs-analysis process and pricing sheet.

checklist.0: Build the commercial-lines process before marketing to that segment.

commonMistakes.0: Treating commercial accounts with the same process as personal lines.

successCriteria.0: First commercial-lines account bound.

aiRecommendations.0: Use industry classification data to identify which commercial verticals to target first.

objectives.0: Add a second producer to grow the commercial-lines book faster.

deliverables.0: A written commission-split agreement for the new producer.

checklist.0: Define the commission-split agreement before the producer's first sale.

commonMistakes.0: Hiring a producer before the commission-split structure is clearly defined.

successCriteria.0: New producer independently binds their first 5 policies.

aiRecommendations.0: Use historical close-rate data to set realistic ramp expectations for the new producer.

objectives.0: Package the book of business for acquisition by a larger agency.

deliverables.0: Every policy, renewal, and claim logged in the agency management system.

checklist.0: Confirm all carrier and client records are complete in the AMS before due diligence.

successCriteria.0: Book of business transfers cleanly with retention intact.

aiRecommendations.0: Use renewal-retention history as evidence of book quality for acquiring firms.

item1.title: Real-estate agents and mortgage lenders are an underused referral source — every home sale needs a new policy.
A single carrier dropping an entire product line can strand a book of clients with no easy replacement option.
Quoting only the carrier with the fastest turnaround instead of the best fit for the client's actual risk.
Offering a free multi-carrier comparison quote converts warm referrals faster than any advertising.
Define the commission-split structure with any new producer in writing before their first sale.
Document every coverage recommendation and every coverage the client declined, in writing, every time.
Agencies that automate renewal re-shopping retain more clients than those that only react when a client calls to shop around.
Clients rarely leave over price alone — slow claims support is the most common reason for switching brokers.
Carrier appointment renewal and continuing-education fees are easy to underbudget in year one.
Demand for bundled home-and-auto and small-commercial coverage is growing faster than demand for single-policy sales.
An independent broker built a 200-policy book in 3 years primarily through real-estate agent referrals.
A brokerage that appointed with only one carrier lost its entire book when that carrier exited the regional market.

q1.question: How many carrier appointments do I need to start?
q1.answer: Most brokers start with 4-5 carrier appointments per line of business to have meaningful comparison options.

myth1.myth: Independent brokers always cost clients more than going direct to an insurer.
myth1.fact: Commission is built into the carrier's premium either way — an independent broker doesn't add a markup, and often finds a better rate through comparison.

item1.title: Review a client's entire coverage picture at every renewal, not just the policy up for renewal.

resources.checklist1: Checklist: Client document-intake checklist by return type

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes a sharp seasonal concentration of revenue, smoothed only as business retainer clients grow.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — break-even is reachable within the first tax season given the low fixed-cost base.

grossMargin.notes: Estimate accounting for seasonal contract-staff costs during peak filing weeks.

netMargin.notes: After tax-software licensing, e-filing fees, and seasonal staffing.

scenarios.conservative: 120 individual returns at the base tier, minimal business retainer income.
scenarios.expected: 200 individual returns plus 5 business retainer clients.
scenarios.optimistic: 300+ individual returns plus 10 business retainer clients on year-round planning.

risks.risk1: Self-service and AI-assisted tax software increasingly automates the simplest, most price-sensitive returns.
risks.risk2: A short season means a bad few weeks (illness, software outage) has an outsized revenue impact.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Model revenue as sharply seasonal, with business retainers as the smoothing mechanism.

positioning.statement: Every deduction found, every deadline met — technical tax expertise without the big-firm price tag.

marketing.brandPromise: Your return, filed accurately and on time, with every deduction you're actually entitled to.

uvp.statement: Enrolled Agent-credentialed tax preparation with the right to represent you directly before the tax authority.
uvp.diff1: Enrolled Agent — licensed to represent clients before the tax authority

icp.description: Individuals with moderately complex returns (self-employment, investments, rental property) and small-business owners needing annual filing.
icp.firmographics: Self-employed individuals and businesses with under 20 employees.

persona1.name: Self-employed freelancer
persona1.description: Freelancer or contractor whose return got too complex for a self-service app.

persona2.name: Small-business owner
persona2.description: Owner needing annual business filing and occasional strategic tax guidance.

painPoints.point1: Worried they're missing deductions they're entitled to.
painPoints.point2: Anxious about filing deadlines and potential penalties.

outcomes.outcome1: Confidence that the return is accurate and maximizes legitimate deductions.
outcomes.outcome2: Peace of mind that the filing deadline will be met without last-minute stress.

messaging.core: Stop guessing at your taxes. File with someone licensed to stand behind every number.
messaging.support1: Enrolled Agent-credentialed, not a seasonal call-center preparer.

brandVoice.tone: Precise, responsive, and reassuring under deadline pressure.

contentPillars.pillar2: Deduction and credit awareness for freelancers and small businesses

marketing.channelStrategy: Referrals from existing clients and local accountants are primary; local-SEO content around filing deadlines builds secondary demand; a year-round newsletter is experimental off-season engagement.

community.description: A referral relationship with local bookkeepers and accountants who don't handle complex individual or business tax filings themselves.

leadMagnets.magnet1: Free "which deductions am I missing" mini-review

stage1.name: Seasonal search or referral

stage2.name: Document intake

objectives.0: Standardize the document-intake-to-e-file workflow.

objectives.0: Announce availability ahead of the upcoming tax season.

item1.title: Converting even a small fraction of seasonal individual clients into year-round business retainers meaningfully smooths cash flow.
A software outage or personal illness during peak season has an outsized impact given how concentrated the revenue window is.
Competing purely on price against self-service software instead of on complexity-handling and representation rights.
A free "which deductions am I missing" mini-review converts warm referrals faster than any paid ad.
Recruit and train seasonal preparers at least 6 weeks before the season, not during the rush.
Set a hard client cap per preparer before the season starts — quality erodes fast past a certain volume.
Practices that send a mid-year tax-planning check-in retain more clients season over season than those that go quiet after filing.
Most clients switch preparers not over price, but after a missed deadline or an unexplained tax-authority notice.
Professional tax-software license renewal fees rise every year and are easy to underbudget.
Demand for representation before the tax authority (audits, notices) is rising as automated filing increases the volume of flagged returns.
A solo Enrolled Agent grew from 50 to 250 individual returns in 3 seasons purely through referrals and local SEO.
A practice that took on too many clients in its first season missed several filing deadlines and lost most of that client base the following year.

q1.question: Can I run this business remotely?
q1.answer: Yes — most document exchange and return preparation can be done fully remotely through a secure client portal.

myth1.myth: AI tax software will fully replace human tax preparers within a few years.
myth1.fact: AI automates simple returns well, but complex situations and tax-authority representation still require a credentialed human.

item1.title: Confirm the actual filing deadline with every client before promising any turnaround time.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Keep all guidance at the level of running/growing the practice — never give specific tax advice for an end client's actual return.
